data.title: American Outdoor Brands: Despite Ambitious Plans, Results Don't Justify A Bullish View
data.description:
One interesting company for investors to be aware of is American Outdoor Brands (NASDAQ:AOUT). With a market capitalization as of this writing of only $161.2 million, the company likely flies under the radar of most investors. Over the last few years now, financial performance for the business has been very mixed, though there are some signs of life brewing. Management seems to have a plan to build long term value for shareholders. However, I would argue that while the company certainly has potential, shares are not priced at levels that are conducive to a ‘buy’ rating. In fact, as things stand, the best rating that I can give the company at the moment is a ‘hold’. And depending on how things go moving forward with the economy, a downgrade is not unthinkable.

An interesting and small firm

According to the management team at American Outdoor Brands, the company focuses on providing goods for a wide array of consumers under the outdoor enthusiast category. Examples here include those interested in hunting, fishing, camping, shooting, outdoor cooking, personal security, and personal defense. The products the business sells include, but are not limited to, premium sportsman knives and tools that are used for fishing and hunting, land management tools for hunting preparedness, harvesting products dedicated to both the hunting and fishing industries, outdoor cooking products, camping, survival, and emergency preparedness products, and more. On top of all of this, the company also produces and sells shooting sports accessories like rests, vaults, electro-optical devices like hunting optics, firearm aiming devices, laser grips, and more. It even sells certain reloading, gunsmithing, and firearm cleaning supplies.

This is a rather diverse pool of products. As expected, the company sells these under a large number of different brand names. And as is common for many businesses today, it has both an online presence and a wholesale division that it calls ‘traditional channels’. Historically speaking, the vast majority of the company's revenue comes from its domestic market here in the US. As an example, during the first nine months of its 2025 fiscal year, the company generated 93.7% of its revenue from here at home. The rest of its revenue comes from miscellaneous international markets.

Over the last few years now, financial performance for the business has been quite mixed. From 2022 to 2023, for instance, revenue for the company plummeted from $247.5 million to $191.2 million. There was then a slight increase in sales to $201.1 million last year. There are a couple of different ways that we can view the revenue the company has generated during this time. When we look at it split between e-commerce and traditional sales, we see a bit of a difference. Both categories reported drops in revenue from 2022 to 2023. But then, in 2024, the firm's e-commerce sales dropped further, declining from $87.2 million to $84.3 million.

Unfortunately, this subsequent drop was driven by a reduction in shooting sports products coming from Amazon (AMZN). But we will get to that data shortly. Traditional sales, meanwhile, rose from $104 million in 2023 to $116.8 million last year. Some of this was because of discounting associated with personal protection products. But most of it was because of higher demand for certain shooting accessories, as well as for hunting and fishing products. Part of this jump in sales can be attributed to the fact that the company began selling one of its outdoor lifestyle direct to consumer only brands to its retail customers.

What's more interesting to me is when we look at the revenue through the lens of its shooting versus its outdoor products. Both of these reported drops from 2022 to 2023. But then, in 2024, they both reported increases. This can be seen in the chart above. One thing that does become clear from this is that most of the pain that the company has seen came from its shooting products. Even though revenue associated with those increased from $88.9 million in 2023 to $91.7 million last year, the figures reported for 2024 still ended up representing a decline of 28.4% compared to what the company reported in 2022.

According to management, even though the company benefited from higher demand for some of its shooting accessories, the decline in revenue experienced from 2022 to 2023 was driven not only by reduced demand for shooting products, but also by the act of the company’s retail customers reducing their own inventories. In an article that I published earlier this year about Sturm, Ruger & Company (RGR), I provided a pretty detailed look at gun ownership trends and background checks data. These results dated all the way back to 1999. In the chart above, you can see that same data that I provided in the aforementioned article. Because of the COVID-19 pandemic and the fact that gun purchases are largely fear motivated, there was a surge in demand a couple of years ago. However, now that the pandemic has come to an end, there has been a normalization in this industry. It's unclear when things will bottom out, or if they have already. But that helps to explain most of the pain that American Outdoor Brands has experienced in that space.

Even though this might not sound like a great thing, I think investors can and should breathe a sigh of relief that the reduction in revenue experienced in the shooting category in recent years can be attributed to market stabilization following a boom as opposed to bad operational strategies or brand weakness by the company. The weakness in the outdoor lifestyle category seems to have been driven largely by a similar boom in sales during the pandemic because of increased access to capital and a desire to engage in more social distancing. But of course, that decline was less pronounced.

On the bottom line, the picture for the business has been all over the map. Because of a $67.8 million impairment charge that the company saw in 2022, net profits were negative to the tune of $64.9 million. But in 2023, the company generated a net loss of $12 million, followed by a $12.2 million loss last year. Cash flows have experienced volatility also. The good news is that, over the last three years, operating cash flow has gotten better from negative $18 million to positive $24.5 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, however, we would get a drop from $29.6 million to $7.9 million. And over the same window of time, EBITDA for the company contracted from $32.2 million to $5.7 million.

For the 2025 fiscal year, we do have data covering the first nine months. During this time, the company reported revenue of $160.4 million. That represents an increase over the $154.8 million reported the previous year. As the chart below illustrates, both traditional sales and e-commerce sales increased during this time. And as the chart below that illustrates, while shooting related revenue still declined, dipping from $67.8 million to $67.5 million, the outdoor lifestyle products for the company reported growth from $87 million to $92.9 million. It seems as though hunting and fishing products sales under its e-commerce business, as well as hunting product sales for the traditional category, served to boost these revenues.

The bottom line for the company has largely improved during this time. For the first nine months of the 2025 fiscal year, the company generated a profit of $0.9 million. That compares to a loss of $6.9 million reported the previous year. Operating cash flow did worsen from $9.8 million to negative $6.4 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an improvement from $8.1 million to $13.4 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business went from $5.7 million to $11.5 million.

Longer term, management seems optimistic, but their optimism centers around the outdoor lifestyle category. According to management, this is a massive market. It totals about 175 million Americans. While the hunters and target shooters are significant at 15 million and 40 million, respectively, these are dwarfed by some of the other categories. This includes the 92 million households that cook outdoors. It also includes 86 million property owners that could benefit from some of the security products the company sells, and the 54 million anglers.

Management has been very open about their objective to continue growing market share in the outdoor lifestyle category. The firm hopes to eventually grow its revenue to around $400 million annually, though they have not set a deadline on when this would be. If they hit that target, they expect that about 65% of revenue will come from the outdoor lifestyle category. That would represent about $260 million in sales. Again, last year, the company generated only $109.4 million from this category of products. By comparison, the shooting sports category should grow more modestly from $91.7 million last year to $140 million. Some of the expansion will come from additional international sales. But even at its peak, the company expects that at least 90% of revenue will come from its domestic market.

For the current fiscal year, management believes that revenue will come in at between $207 million and $210 million. And for 2026, they expect a further increase to between $220 million and $230 million. The company has not provided profitability guidance for 2026. But they do expect a loss per share of between $0.19 and $0.27 this year. They also expect EBITDAS (which is EBITDA, plus adding back stock-based compensation) of between $14.5 million and $15.5 million. At the midpoint, this would likely translate to EBITDA of around $8.7 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $12.1 million.

Using these estimates, as well as the historical performance that the company achieved last year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Honestly, these are multiples that are much more in alignment with a ‘hold’ rating than they are a ‘buy’ rating. Furthermore, compared to other similar companies, the stock looks pretty much fairly valued. In the table below, I compared it with five similar firms. And what I found was that, in both valuation cases, three of the five companies ended up being cheaper than American Outdoor Brands is.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
American Outdoor Brands 13.3 16.6
Outdoor Holding Co (POWW) 11.9 91.7
Acushnet Holdings Co (GOLF) 19.3 13.8
Sturm, Ruger & Company 10.6 8.3
YETI Holdings (YETI) 9.4 8.4
Clarus Corporation (CLAR) 17.3 245.4

Takeaway

As interesting as American Outdoor Brands happens to be, I just don't see a reason to be terribly optimistic in the near term. Management has a clear plan to grow the business, but it's likely to take many years in order to achieve. The business is certainly not expected to grow rapidly in the near term and shares are priced at levels at are solidly fairly valued. This is true on both an absolute basis and relative to similar firms. The good news is that the company deserves a little bit of wiggle room because it has no debt on its books and enjoys a net cash position of $17.1 million. But that's not enough to justify anything better than a ‘hold’ rating at this time.

data.title: IBIT: The ETF That Owns Over 3% Of Bitcoin
data.description:
Introduction

While it's not the first exchange-traded fund that got approval to offer Bitcoin (BTC-USD) exposure to investors, the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (NASDAQ:IBIT) is the largest at a monstrous $65.6B in assets, amounting to over 636,000 Bitcoins. This is a little over 3% of the total 21 million Bitcoins that will ever exist, including those that haven't been mined yet.

The fund launched in January 2024, and sits among the largest ETFs on the market, ranking in the top 30 by assets under management ("AUM").

What's even crazier is that Bitcoin funds like IBIT have become so popular that they now encompass over 1% of all ETF assets.

Owning Bitcoin through funds like IBIT has become so popular among investors that Bitcoin funds now own over 6% of total supply, half of which is IBIT itself, even more than businesses like (Micro)Strategy (MSTR) and MARA Holdings (MARA) and world governments combined, and more than the creator of Bitcoin, Satoshi Nakamoto.

IBIT Overview

The fund itself is fairly straightforward to understand: it owns Bitcoin and benefits from the price fluctuations in the asset. Except for the management fee, in IBIT's case 0.25% p.a. taken from the fund's assets daily to reduce friction, and shown net in performance metrics, the fund is supposed to directly track the price of Bitcoin.

Remember that IBIT only trades during market hours, since it is an ETF, while Bitcoin trades on exchanges 24/7, so the prices won't match up perfectly during off-hours. However, the two assets typically move together over longer periods of time, and the difference between them since IBIT's launch has been very slight. This is mostly due to fees paid and tracking error, where the fund doesn't follow the price exactly due to discounts/premiums forming in the ETF structure, the drag of holding some cash, and the timing of purchases.

The fund does not distribute anything, and its return is made entirely of capital gains in its price return. This is handy for investors because it makes IBIT fairly tax friendly, where futures-based strategies tend to distribute lots of ordinary income.

We can see how closely the fund tracks Bitcoin in its correlation, which is near-perfect, albeit with some discrepancies, particularly when Bitcoin is trading dramatically after market hours, and IBIT changes dramatically at market open to compensate for the overnight moves. This is most-clearly evident in the April 2025 flash-crash, where the correlation decoupled to a low of 0.924 (1 being a perfect correlation) before bouncing back toward 1 again.

IBIT Holdings

As mentioned in the previous section, IBIT owns Bitcoin outright instead of futures or any other proxy method of gaining exposure to the price movements of Bitcoin. When looking at the holdings, they are the most straightforward I have ever seen in an ETF.

They have two positions: BTC and USD, or Bitcoin and Cash.

This is as basic as it gets for ETFs, which is a boon to IBIT shareholders because it makes it easy to see how much the fund owns, and there is no obfuscation behind futures or other proxy assets.

One of the things to consider for investors is that ETFs tend to trade at a discount or premium, based on the demand for shares. Because the fund doesn't buy Bitcoin every time that shares are traded, there can be some discrepancy between the net-asset value ("NAV") and the AUM. These discounts/premiums are usually slight, and fleeting, as ETFs have processes for eliminating them. These include using authorized participants to perform arbitrage on the fund, most notably. Investors don't need to worry about this factor too much, but it should be noted before purchases, in case there are extreme movements in this figure.

These typically form during periods of market stress, and can happen without warning. This is true of all ETFs, but is more common among volatile funds like Bitcoin-holding ETFs.

The Logistics of Owning Bitcoin

There is a lot to consider when buying digital assets, particularly the question: where do you store them? Security is a huge concern in the cryptocurrency world, and it is rife with scams and failed exchanges that have left investors with little of their digital treasure left, see Mt. Gox and FTX to name just two.

Anyone who has tried to, or successfully, owned Bitcoin themselves will know that it can be a hassle, and that is more and more true the less "techy" one is. There is a lot of room for human error in doing transactions, moving coins from one place to another, and there are potential security risks such as losing the keys to your wallet, and thus the ability to access your Bitcoin holdings, that make holding your own personal Bitcoin a subpar option for many folks.

Enter ETFs like IBIT, that take a lot of the complexity and concern out of the equation. The fund uses Coinbase Global Inc. (COIN) as its custodian, utilizing their Coinbase Prime service to physically hold the Bitcoin on its balance sheet. Using this method, IBIT doesn't have to employ a "Bitcoin strategy" like its peers where it uses futures to approximate Bitcoin exposure. It owns the coins outright, dramatically reducing the barriers for individuals to own Bitcoin, as they are able to own it by proxy through owning shares of the fund, with only the management fee as a drag.

IBIT handles all the logistics of owning Bitcoin for investors. It is traded just like a normal ETF, although it is not an ordinary ETF per se. iShares classifies it as an "ETP" or an exchange-traded product. This is because IBIT holds Bitcoin directly, and so owns a commodity, per the legal definition of Bitcoin. IBIT isn't a commodity trading advisor ("CTA") or commodity pool either, but trades as its own security under the Securities Act. This last part isn't necessarily important to shareholders, because the fund acts just like any other ETF in practice, but bears note because of the warning on the IBIT website discussing the differences.

Why Bitcoin?

All of this is great, but it begs the question: should you own Bitcoin at all?

The answers are mixed, and it really depends on who you ask. iShares has several talking points that they share in their product brief for IBIT that are relevant to potential investors.

The vast majority of cryptocurrency investors I've spoken with cite global adoption of the asset still being "in the works," the futuristic nature of the technology and its use cases (many of which, I am personally dubious about), its independence from governments, among other reasons.

One point they all tend to share is that it has had massive returns over its lifetime. From 2014 to 2024, it was either the best or worst performing asset in every year, with a cumulative 11,836% over that time, compared to the market's 293%, gold's 114%, and bond's 21%.

This chart does show how risky Bitcoin can be as well, with its worst years all being over 50% losses, with its worst annualized return in 2018 at -74%.

It is also a technology that has moved very quickly, with its user acquisition being faster than mobile phones or the Internet itself.

This article is certainly not exhaustive on Bitcoin, but it is intended to be an overview of IBIT and not necessarily Bitcoin itself. It is up to the individual investor whether they find Bitcoin attractive or not for investment.

IBIT vs. Peers

The first Bitcoin ETF was actually a "Bitcoin Strategy" ETF, BITO, which means that it invests in Bitcoin futures to gain its exposure to Bitcoin, and not the spot price, as is the case with newer Bitcoin ETFs. This strategy comes with a lot of drawbacks, because derivatives like futures do not always track their reference assets very well. Once proper Bitcoin ETFs like IBIT debuted, a lot of money left BITO and other "strategy" ETFs to mitigate these issues. Because BITO distributes almost all of its return, it is tax inefficient. The fund is from an era before proper spot Bitcoin ETFs, so it will be excluded from this comparison.

There are a ton of funds in the digital asset space, and Seeking Alpha lists over 89 (49 of which are covered by Quant), but many of them have very few assets under management, and can be somewhat illiquid at times. Investors should be very careful in this space, especially if they intend to invest a large amount.

The primary competition, judged by AUM and volume, are Fidelity's FBTC, Grayscale's GBTC, and ARK Invest's ARKB. Even then, IBIT stands as a monolith in the space, with triple the assets of FBTC, the second largest spot Bitcoin fund.

ETFs trade with a bid/ask process, and the more volume, the tighter the spread is, which means better pricing for investors when they purchase shares. This is why investors may prefer IBIT over these other funds, especially the larger one's investment is, as that may make liquidity more or less important.

All of them have done very similar jobs in tracking Bitcoin's price, with the differences being very slight.

From an expense ratio perspective, ARKB has the lowest ER at 0.21%, versus IBIT and FBTC's tie at 0.25%.

Over IBIT's lifetime, GBTC has been the best performer (even exceeding Bitcoin's price return), and ARKB the worst, although the difference is likely neglibible to most investors over time. These performance metrics are not indicative of future performance, of course, and are likely laid out the way they are due to simple timing.

IBIT Pros & Cons

When comparing the pros and cons of IBIT, it's important to consider the alternatives. There are fewer pros and cons when it comes to other physical Bitcoin ETFs like FBTC, where they both have identical strategies and the only real advantage of IBIT is its size and volume.

However, the fund definitely has clear pros and cons over self-custody, previously one of the most common form of Bitcoin investment prior to the launch of ETFs, other than holding them through exchanges like Coinbase (COIN) and Robinhood (HOOD).

Pros Cons
IBIT Liquid, tax efficient, low complexity. Tracking error, management fees.
Self-Custody No reliance on institutions, no fees. High complexity, potential for catastrophic failure.
On-Exchange Spot pricing, ability to earn interest via lending. More complicated taxes.

Suitability

Through all of it this, it's important to remember that Bitcoin is still a very volatile and speculative asset in itself. This means IBIT can come with significant drawdowns. Its own history doesn't show this well because IBIT was launched after the worst drawdowns in BTC's history, and we haven't seen a repeat of those since (no promises either way).

Looking at this next chart, showing the percentage off highs for Bitcoin, should give investors pause. Consider this asset very carefully.

This is not an asset for the weak-stomached, and investors need to consider what they would do if the asset fell over 80% from their purchase price. If the answer is "sell it and never touch it again," your risk tolerance is likely too low for Bitcoin.

Typically, when I advise investors regarding cryptocurrencies, I tend to give conservative advice, so take these allocation levels with a grain of salt. Note that these are not an endorsement of the fund, per se. To follow this guidance, one has to already have decided on an IBIT investment independently.

Aggressive investors could consider up to a 3% allocation to Bitcoin via IBIT, ensuring that they are properly diversified in the rest of their portfolio, especially if investors also have exposure to companies that mine or hold Bitcoin directly.

Moderate investors could consider up to a 1% allocation to IBIT with the understanding that it is likely on the cusp of their risk tolerance.

Conservative investors may avoid IBIT and all Bitcoin-related funds, as their risk tolerance is far too low for the kinds of volatiltiy that Bitcoin has seen in the past and will likely endure in the future.

One line I find worth remembering when it comes to asset allocation, and doubly so when it comes to risk-on assets like IBIT:

Position sizing is often more important than security selection.

Conclusion

The iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (NASDAQ:IBIT) is the largest and most liquid Bitcoin fund. It holds Bitcoin directly, giving investors an alternative to self-custody or on-exchange ownership of Bitcoin.

IBIT allows investors access to Bitcoin from within their normal brokerage accounts, and come with all the bells and whistles of an ordinary ETF, which has its distinct advantages over other methods, namely in its simplicty and liquidity. It does come with some drawbacks, of course, but the fund has a successful history of tracking Bitcoin's performance with only a slight bit of error expected of ETFs.

Of course, none of that speaks to Bitcoin's investment thesis, and investors are cautioned that Bitcoin, and subsequently IBIT, since it derives all of its value from Bitcoin, is a very risky asset that deserves scrutiny before it is added to one's portfolio.

Thanks for reading.

This article answers these three main questions about IBIT:

  1. Is IBIT more or less volatile than equity ETFs?
  2. How closely does IBIT track the price of the underlying Bitcoin?
  3. Does IBIT make it easier for investors to gain exposure to crypto investments?
Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: DCR Tech Group: Too Early To Buy, Too Interesting To Ignore
data.description:
It’s not every day you come across an IPO for a company whose main gig is producing visual content in China. Definitely not your typical listing.

To cut some corners, it’s easier to say what it’s not first—that way we’ll set the record straight.

It works on Chinese OTT productions, but only with visual effects. So we’re not talking about iQIYI (IQ), also known as the ‘Netflix of China’, or Youku, part of Alibaba’s (BABA) group.

They also create visual effects for film and television—but not directly. So we’re also not talking about their own content production companies like Huace Film or China Television Media.

Besides those two main areas (which make up the bulk of its contracts), the company also dabbles in a few other segments — including gaming, animation, digital cultural tourism, advertising, and extended reality.

Well, I doubt you've thought of DCR Tech Group (DCR), a nearly four-year-old company operating in China through its operating subsidiary.

DCR works end-to-end in the visual content creation process.

For example: a company hires them to prepare the visual content for a television advertisement. The company itself puts together the storyboard, working until the final visualization of the content and delivery of the service.

This puts the company right in the middle of three major market niches — each growing at its own pace:

  • China VFX Market: is estimated to be worth approximately $106.6 billion in 2024, projected to reach over $344 billion by the year 2032 (growing at a CAGR of approximately 16%);
  • China OTT Video Streaming Market: There is little information available on its total size, with some data from 2023 suggesting that the three largest platforms together have a pie worth over $10 billion. PwC China estimates that it is expected to grow at a CAGR of 7.4% through 2027. However, we do have some data that suggests it has over 750 million subscribers (with Tencent Video and iQIYI together reaching over 200 million households);
  • Online Advertising Visual Production in China: This was $53.4 million in size last year and is projected to reach $145.3 million by 2030, growing at a CAGR of 18% over that period. Within this, video advertising (where DCR works) already represents 27% of the category’s total revenue.

And while almost all of these niches are showing double-digit CAGRs, DCR hasn’t seen a huge increase from FY 2023 to FY 2024 (in fact, the company had 16 clients in FY 2023 and 17 clients in FY 2024).

But here’s the catch. The scope of these projects has expanded significantly in recent years. Just look at the revenue stream.

They simply saw their revenues skyrocket from $5.53 million to $15.86 million (an impressive 180% year-over-year growth).

Another catch here: if you look at the six months ending December 31, 2024 (where the first half of FY 2025 ends) you will see even stronger revenue growth, but not only that. They served 31 customers (in this same period of FY 2024, the six months ending December 31, 2023), you would see only 9 customers in DCR's account.

This tells us that in just six months they served almost twice as many customers as in FY 2024!

This is quite significant and shows that the business is scaling quickly. In terms of revenue, DCR generated $15.03 million in the six months ending December 31, 2024, growing at least 177% year-over-year.

Looking at margins, DCR delivered a gross margin of 14.6% in FY 2023, growing just under 200 bp year-over-year to 16.1% in FY 2024. In H1 FY 2025 the company stretched it a bit further to 17.4%. The same goes for EBIT, which was $0.48 million in FY 2023, growing 195% year-over-year to $1.42 million (a margin that went from 8.6% to 8.9%). In H1 2025 they increased it to $1.2 million, almost the same as in all of FY 2024, with margins of almost 8%.

This reduction in margins came from SG&A expenses that were five times higher year-over-year, likely coming from higher IPO preparation charges. Normalizing this to something around $100,000, we would see an EBIT margin of approximately 8.5% or higher.

So, there is nothing unusual about this sudden drop in the EBIT margin.

For FY 2023, DCR reported net income of $0.4 million (an EPS of around $0.01 and a net margin of 7.2%), growing 198% year-over-year to $1.19 million in FY 2024 (EPS of $0.02 and a net margin of 7.5%). In the first half of FY 2025, net income grew 202% year-over-year to $1.08 million (EPS of $0.03 and a net margin of 7.2%).

Remember, net income here was also impacted by higher SG&A expenses, so everything looks normal, even with the decline in margins.

Now, looking at the cash flow, you can see the main reason behind this IPO: working capital. It is no wonder that they intend to allocate the bulk of the money raised to this item.

If you notice, DCR generates strong cash flow supported by its net income. However, there is strong volatility in operating assets and liabilities, reducing it and increasing the need for cash.

A very simple practical example. If we excluded the changes in operating assets, in H1 FY 2025 DCR would have generated an OCF of $1.14 million (growing approximately 206% year over year). However, they saw a headwind of over $3 million in accounts receivable and advances to suppliers. This caused a negative OCF of $1.4 million in the quarter.

Business is like that. Depending on where you operate (and where you are in the market), you have to make some concessions to grow, it's all part of the game.

With irrelevant financing and investment cash flows, DCR generated a cash deficit of $1.4 million, reducing its cash position to just $374,431 at the end of the half.

Now, if DCR wants to continue growing healthily, it is more than necessary to obtain a source of financing (and preferably a cheap one). It is the classic story of a company that grows in triple digits year after year and begins to be bombarded by operating assets that suffocate its cash flow.

In a catastrophic scenario, DCR could compensate for this through short-term debt, but this is payable in less than a year and hinders the possibility of long-term planning. This is called the 'Fleuriet Effect' or 'Scissors Effect' in the literature.

This happens in large families too. Dutch Bros (BROS), a drive-thru coffee shop that is growing very quickly, went through this four years ago. Receivables rise, eating up all the OCF, investment needs skyrocket and you are at the mercy of short-term debt.

In the case in question, the company made an IPO and obtained a long-term credit line at attractive interest rates. It worked and today it is one of the most successful companies in the industry... and also one of my recommendations that has taken off the most.

DCR is at this inflection point.

It couldn't last another quarter without funding, and the way it's growing, equity could be its salvation.

Bottom line? DCR is growing fast — and more importantly, it's doing so profitably. But none of that would matter if the IPO came in at an unreasonable price.

The Offer: How Much Is This Growth Worth?

DCR intends to issue 2 million shares at a price of $4 to $6. Considering the midpoint of this offering, this is a deal worth approximately $10 million.

Some additional details include:

  1. All 2 million shares issued will be Class A. Class A and Class B shares will retain the same economic rights, but Class B shares (which will remain in the hands of the founders and insiders) are entitled to twenty votes, while Class A shares are entitled to only one vote;
  2. Following the IPO, DCR will have approximately 42 million shares outstanding (24.9 million Class A shares and 17.1 million Class B shares);
  3. After deducting the issuance costs, DCR will raise $8.21 million. They intend to allocate 50% of this to working capital, 30% to R&D and 20% to talent acquisition/training.

To take a look at the multiple, we need to make some assumptions about FY 2025. To make this process easier, I will assume that the year-over-year growth seen in H1 FY 2025 continues at the same strength for the entire year. Thus, we will have the following forecasts:

  • Revenue of $44 million. A revenue per share of $1.10;
  • An EBIT of $10.2 million;
  • A net income of $3.7 million. This would give an EPS of approximately $0.08.

Using the midpoint of the offering, DCR would be trading at a P/S multiple of 4.54x (3.63x at $4). Comparing to other companies (a bit of a daunting task to find), we would see Cineverse (CNVS) with a P/S of 0.7x and the median for the 'Communications and Services' segment at a P/S of 1.16x.

With an EV of just over $122 million, DCR would be trading at an EV/EBIT of 11.96x. Note that when we consider DCR's capital structure, the stock looks cheap, trading at least 24% below the industry median.

Looking at it from an EPS perspective, the implied P/E at $5 would be something close to 62.5x. It doesn't sound cheap, but once we adjust for 200%+ year-over-year growth, we'd have a PEG of 0.3x. At $4, the company could deliver a P/E of 50x and a PEG of 0.25.

Note that everything will depend on how they manage to scale, and at the same time maintain margins at stable levels in FY 2024.

Are we sure that DCR would be able to maintain this? Not at all. The most likely scenario is to count on sales growth, and based on the projections, this would still not give us a satisfactory P/S for the purchase. Keep in mind that the performance of this type of stock (Asian microcaps) is usually weak in the post-listing period until it finds a foothold.

However, DCR seems to me to be an interesting company to keep an eye on. It would certainly be a worthwhile purchase if the classic post-IPO sell-off happens and we can buy it at $3 or below.

That said, my rating on DCR's IPO is 'Hold' (keep an eye on the IPO), but with a more positive outlook than usual.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Alibaba's AI Ambitions Are Promising, But There Are Better AI Stocks
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding Limited (NYSE:BABA)’s stock corrected ~7% immediately after March earnings, and it is still down more than 7% at the time of writing. Despite this, I believe Alibaba’s earnings were quite good, showing early signs of a turnaround and the start of a possible new growth story.

I think the market overreacted to a relatively meaningless miss on both top and bottom line. Financial results are not the issue with BABA, in my view. The problem is, as always, the omnipresent political risk tied to investing in China.

With rising tensions between China, the US and the West, and an increasingly polarized world, I believe there is still too much risk to invest in speculative Chinese companies - it does not matter whether they are cheap according to traditional valuation metrics. While Alibaba’s results are promising I think there are far better and safer AI stocks in the market.

Quarterly earnings show the early signs of a turnaround

For the first time since I started my coverage of Alibaba, I finally noticed some promising signs of business turnaround in March Quarterly earnings. The company was struggling until recently, in my view, with flat eCommerce growth and sub-par growth in Cloud when compared to peers.

Finally, Alibaba is showing signs of timid business acceleration. Overall revenue is growing at 7%, up from the low single digits registered for the past few quarters. Taobao and Tmall, the core eCommerce business of BABA, are growing at 9%, while Cloud accelerated to 18% year-on-year.

I do not think these figures are impressive just yet. A supposed tech company in the AI space, in my view, should showcase far higher growth rates - especially for what concerns its Cloud division. However, I do recognize that the company is finally showing signs of a turnaround and could build an interesting growth story if management can grow from here.

I believe that Alibaba’s growth in Cloud - now a key revenue driver - can be attributed to the overall acceleration in demand for AI solutions. This is benefiting a vast array of companies, and BABA has been able to capture some of that demand. This is what I will cover next.

How Alibaba is playing the AI race

As a keen observer of the AI space, I have noted multiple times in my work on Seeking Alpha how one of the ultimate objectives of AI is to replace human input in the labor market. This, however, will not be a linear process.

I believe AI to be a phenomenon that comes in waves, with different applications at different stages. The image above outlines how AI is coming at different “levels”, starting with relatively limited Chatbot and text-based solutions and evolving until a future where AI can take over the entire workforce in a company.

I believe we are currently between Level 2 and Level 3 of the chart above, rapidly evolving to the stage of AI agents, which are systems that can already automate a set of actions that may normally take one or more employees to perform. Many public companies are already rolling-out AI agents - something I will cover in my next section and I expect we should enter fully Level 3 in the next months.

In the table below, I summarized some of Alibaba’s current product offering for what concerns AI solutions, with comments about what the product is exactly and whether it is monetized, and how.

Product Name

Description

Monetization

Tongyi Qianwen (Qwen Series)

A family of large language models (LLMs) including Qwen-Max, Qwen-Plus and other Qwen LLMs, supporting text generation, multimodal tasks, coding, and reasoning.

Monetized through Alibaba Cloud’s Platform via API call fees, model training, and deployment service charges.

Tongyi Wanxiang

A generative AI model for image generation using diffusion technology, enhanced by Alibaba’s Composer model based on text prompts.

Monetized through Alibaba Cloud’s Modelscope via SaaS fees for businesses.

Wan 2.1-FLF2V-14B

An AI video generation model supporting text-to-image and text-to-video generation, released in April 2025.

Not monetized; offered free on Alibaba Cloud to drive cloud service subscriptions.

Quanzhantui

AI-powered marketing solution enhancing merchants’ ad campaign efficiency and targeting on Taobao and Tmall.

Monetized through software service fees charged to merchants on Taobao and Tmall.

AI-Powered Sourcing Engine

A conversational B2B sourcing tool launched by AIDC in September 2024, matching buyers with suppliers using voice or text prompts.

Monetized through transaction fees and platform service charges on Alibaba’s international platforms.

Lingma

A coding copilot powered by Qwen models, offering intelligent code generation, multi-file code modification, and AI chat for developers.

Monetized via usage-based fees for developers on Alibaba Cloud.

AliMe

An AI-powered customer service chatbot using speech recognition and deep learning, handling over 2 million daily sessions on platforms like Taobao.

Monetized indirectly by reducing human customer service costs on Alibaba platforms.

E-commerce Brain

A system using real-time data to predict consumer preferences and provide personalized product recommendations on Taobao and Tmall.

Monetized through advertising fees from merchants and higher conversion rates.

Ali Smart Supply Chain (ASSC)

An AI-driven tool for supply chain management, forecasting demand, optimizing inventory, and determining pricing strategies.

Monetized through service fees charged to merchants using Alibaba’s platforms.

City Brain

A cloud-based AI system for urban management, optimizing traffic flow and city operations, reducing traffic jams by 15% in Hangzhou.

Monetized through contracts and service fees from municipal governments.

Tmall Smart Selection

An AI algorithm using deep learning to recommend products and communicate inventory needs to retailers during high-traffic events.

Monetized through merchant fees for platform services and increased sales from optimized recommendations.

Platform for AI [PAI]

A machine learning platform offering data preprocessing, model training, deployment, and evaluation tools like PAI-Designer.

Monetized through subscription and usage-based fees for cloud-based AI development services.

Modelscope

A SaaS-like platform providing access to pre-trained generative AI models for businesses to develop custom applications.

Monetized through service fees for access to models and cloud infrastructure on Alibaba Cloud.

Overall, I think Alibaba is playing a good game in the AI space, with monetization starting to come through mostly via their “Cloud” division, which includes most of the AI applications listed above.

However, an 18% year-on-year growth on Cloud is not what I would like to see for a leader in the AI space. Based on March Quarter figures of 9% revenue growth, Alibaba’s PE/G ratio is about 1.4X. Based on earnings growth of ~23%, Alibaba's PE/G ratio is ~0.5X.

While reasonable in absolute terms, other established companies are registering similar growth, while having little political risk in them. Together with exposure to the China domestic market, this less-than-impressive AI growth is the reason why I am maintaining a SELL rating on the stock. I ultimately think there are better AI plays out there.

A note about shares buybacks and PE/G ratios

Alibaba bulls often mention stock repurchases as one of the key reasons why the company is worthy of an investment. Management has been vocal about how much they care about buybacks. In March earnings, the second page of the presentation was fully dedicated to stock repurchases. The company bought back more than 5% of shares in its last fiscal year.

Personally, I would prefer to see management focusing on accelerating a turnaround of the business, rather than deploying so many resources on buybacks. Ultimately, I do not think any company can “repurchase itself” to greatness. Without a thriving core business, buybacks are pointless, in my view. I think a true bull case in Alibaba can only manifest if its political risk vanishes or gets significantly reduced (something I will cover in my risk section).

For what concerns Alibaba's PE/G ratio, I have reported this figure both based on revenue and on earnings growth. Traditionally, a PE/G ratio takes into consideration earnings growth, not revenue growth- and Alibaba is indeed very cheap based on earnings growth at 0.5X (vs. 1.4X on revenue). However, I believe both figures should be considered when evaluating an investment in the AI space. Being a CAPEX-intense sector, I think a PE/G ratio based on revenue trails ahead of one based on earnings growth. As AI companies invest heavily in AI (which has a negative effect on earnings and cash flow in the short term), AI-fueled revenue growth manifests before earnings growth. Considering both figures, I believe, provides a better overall picture on the business.

What AI stocks I would pick over Alibaba

I see plenty of options for investors that want to gain exposure to the AI sector. Alibaba, in my view, is not one of the best, even net of the political risk tied to investing in China and the weakness of the domestic Chinese market.

As this article is based on Alibaba, I won’t spend too much time explaining how other companies are, in my view, better playing the AI race. I will however list three companies that showcase similar or higher growth figures to BABA but have products and investment thesis that are in my opinion better than those of Alibaba. For the sake of a fair comparison, I will also limit myself to companies that are mature, as Alibaba is, and do not represent high risk, asymmetric investment bets.

Starting with NVIDIA Corporations (NVDA), growing overall revenue at a staggering 78% year-on-year (Seeking Alpha data, Q1 2025) and currently valued at a PE/G ratio of ~0.4X on revenue and ~1.3X based on earnings growth. Nvidia’s core business is providing what I think are the “picks and shovels” of the AI race (GPUs), and it is one of the main companies that first benefits from AI demand.

While Nvidia is exposed to China, I see its “China risk” as not comparable to that of Alibaba in the slightest. First and foremost, the company is American based. Secondly, it sells globally and China only represents about $ 15 Billion in sales for the company. Nvidia’s very reasonable PE/G valuation makes it for me a no brainer pick over Alibaba.

Another AI pick I would prefer over Alibaba is Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), which I covered recently, analyzing its balance sheet. The company is more expensive than Alibaba, at a PE/G ratio of ~2.6X based on last quarter’s 13% revenue growth and a FWD P/E of ~34x (Seeking Alpha data), and ~2.3X based on earnings growth. However, I see this premium more than justified by the fact that Microsoft is not a Chinese company, as well as because Microsoft is partnering with Open AI and has a huge moat in terms of rolling up and monetizing AI, thanks to Windows, Office and AWS. While Nvidia is cheaper than BABA and far less risky, Microsoft is a company with an exceptional track record which I believe deserves its premium valuation over BABA.

The last AI stock I would prefer over Alibaba is Alphabet, Inc. (GOOGL), which I also covered recently. Despite assigning a HOLD rating to this stock, and not having direct exposure to it in my portfolio, I still prefer it over Alibaba. I think Google shares one characteristic with Alibaba: it is cheap. At a FWD P/E of just below ~18 at the time of writing (Seeking Alpha data), and 12% Q1 revenue growth, the company features a PE/G ratio of just 1.5X based on revenue, and 1.1X based on earnings growth. In my view, the market discounts Google because of the AI threat to its Search cash cow. Personally, I would rather bet on Google managing to fend off this threat, than on the Chinese economy somehow opening to the West and political risk in China to permanently decrease. In short, if readers want a cheap AI stock, I would rather recommend Google than Alibaba.

Risks to my thesis

Since I have started my Seeking Alpha coverage on Alibaba, I have always argued how the “elephant in the room” with an investment in this company is political risk. The main risk to my thesis, today as always, is that the Chinese Communist Party pivots to a more open political and economic stance towards the West. More broadly, any news related to de-escalating tensions between China and the West (even if initiated by the West) could also act like a catalyst and trigger a stock rally.

Should political risk in China disappear or significantly reduce, I think there would be a major bull market for all Chinese stocks. Alibaba, as a prominent Tech stock, would benefit disproportionately, in my view.

Other risks to my bear thesis include general volatility and turning market sentiment. Alibaba performed decently in the past few months, albeit below the Nasdaq and only after a prolonged bear market (the company still sits ~60% below its ATH in 2021). While the fundamentals of the company (and political risk) did not change, market sentiment together with significant shares buybacks still managed to sustain BABA’s share price in the last months.

Conclusion

Alibaba has been fascinating investors for years because of how “cheap” it is. Buying a company just because it is cheap is, in my view, generally a mistake. However, Alibaba bulls also tend to argue that the company is not only cheap, but it is also an emerging global contender in the AI race. That, I recognize after March earnings, is true.

The problem is that I believe there are far better AI stocks in the market. Companies that are cheaper or similarly priced as Alibaba, but that, contrary to Alibaba, have no (or limited) political risk tied to them. Companies that, contrary to Alibaba, have a stronger track record of double digit growth and rolling out AI-related innovation to customers.

If readers are fascinated by the cheap valuation of Alibaba, I recommend they rather look at Nvidia, which is trading at a discount relative to its history and it is a proven AI company with an outstanding track record.

If readers are intrigued by Alibaba’s AI-fueled Cloud growth, I recommend they rather look at Microsoft or Google, both big global players in the Cloud space. In the case of Google, readers can also find a “comeback” story similar to that of Alibaba - with the difference that said comeback can happen as a result of management’s execution and not politics.

Personally, I continue assigning a “SELL” rating to BABA. I do not see a company that can generate alpha, as it remains entangled in political risk in an ever more polarized world. While it’s showing promising results in AI, I think there are cheaper and/or better AI companies to invest in.

data.title: LKQ Corporation: Quietly Crushing It Again
data.description:
Summary

I’m going to start off by cutting to the chase - LKQ Corporation (NASDAQ:LKQ) still put up some solid numbers last quarter. Their size and market positioning are really helping them out in both North America and Europe. Even though the whole industry might face some profit challenges down the road, I think LKQ still looks pretty good at current price levels when you consider their strong history and size. They're also making smart moves with acquisitions and using tech to stay competitive. Plus, they're giving back to shareholders with dividends and buybacks, which is why I'm sticking with a Buy rating. The key thesis points I made in my original article still hold and as a reminder they include:

  • Undervalued from a historic basis

  • Strong historical financial performance and balance sheet

  • Promising growth prospects based on a size and scale advantage

There Is Still Financial Performance and Revenue Growth, Based On What We Can See

In 1Q25, LKQ showed us they can still put out solid numbers despite a rapidly changing environment, particularly in North America. Both North American and European segments benefited from organic growth initiatives and strategic market positioning, favorable FX rates helped boost LKQ's top-line numbers, contributing to overall revenue growth. On a per share basis, revenue still look very solid for what you get.

“North America also benefited from having a diversified portfolio of products and services, mitigating potential shortfalls in other business areas. We generated positive growth for our lead tech, calibration, and diagnostics business, and our bumper-to-bumper hard parts business in Canada.” - Justin Jude - President and CEO

LKQ currently maintains respectable profit margins while expanding market share, although future periods could look uglier. What does this mean for LKQ stock? I’ll elaborate more once I go through my model assumption changes and valuation methodology, later in this article. However, if you’re unwilling to scroll down further, here is what you need to know - the swift changes underpinning the industry are going to be an overall profit killer - I know this, you know this, the market knows this. The market does tend to overreact and I believe the price is now very attractive relative to where performance is expected to land for the rest of FY25. Things are changing so fast that when I first started writing this, things looked pretty grim and then we got the auto sector tariff exemption. That doesn’t change my view that LKQ is a solid performer, is well run and possesses a very valuable size and scale advantage.

Strategic Acquisitions and Market Expansion

LKQ isn't merely aiming for incremental gains but is and has been pursuing a strategy that leverages its size and scale advantage. Strategic acquisitions have been a component of LKQ's growth trajectory, contributing to revenue growth, although not all growth is organic. These are often calculated decisions, aimed at expanding the LKQ's operational capabilities and strengthening its supply chain.

By integrating strategic acquisitions, LKQ can improve its market position and strengthen its competitive edge, potentially making life harder for competitors. To improve overall efficiency and enhance products, management is surgically integrating these new components. By doing this, LKQ strengthens its competitive position, making it harder for others to compete.

Technological Advancements and Innovation

Technology is a part of enhancing operational capabilities and keeping a competitive edge, a theme observed across various companies and is not specific to LKQ. It's common for companies to invest in technology and digital platforms to boost efficiency. Arguments can be made whether this will boost LKQ's position or maintain it, but the fact that they're doing it keeps them in the game relative to others in the space. Staying proactive in technological advancements is essential for long-term sustainability and growth.

Thinking Optimistically, The Stock Price Can Still Go Up If Earnings Underperform Because……

LKQ has historically returned value in other ways via a combination of dividends and stock repurchases. In 1Q25, LKQ declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.30 per share, payable on March 27th, and subsequently declared another dividend of the same amount payable on May 29th. Additionally, LKQ has been actively repurchasing its shares, with a total of ~1 million shares repurchased in 1Q25. LKQ still has a substantial authorization remaining for future repurchases, with approximately $1.7B available through October 2026.

For 1Q25 Earnings Estimates, I Give Myself A B+

As you can see above, my 1Q estimate was optimistic. The world was a lot different back when I last updated this model, and for being +/-10% within print with all that has gone on in the past few months is nothing short of a miracle, in my opinion. That also means management guides pretty well, so I can reasonably follow what management spews out as a forecasting benchmark.

Above is a snippet of my estimates, where I tried to follow towards the low end of management guidance. I didn’t quite get to management’s guide and I think the assumptions I have in there currently are reasonable. We also now have a bridge to adjusted EPS figures! I’m not a big fan of modeling to an adjusted figure, but that is the number cited by management and leads to a Who’s on First bit if you don’t at least measure it - I’m using it as a sense check for rest of 2025 assumptions. As I’ve noted in my other pieces, I tend to model conservatively and look for ways to break my thesis and assumptions, as opposed to making outlandish claims (TSLA $3,000 price target, anyone?).

We Are Undervalued Territory

When I first wrote about LKQ, my price target was in the high $60s. As I’ve mentioned here and in other articles, boy was the world much different then. Nonetheless, the number I’m getting to is now somewhere in the high $50s based on the changes in assumptions. Had I actually been able to get to management’s guidance figures, the stock should be valued a little higher. All in all, given current trading levels in the $40s, pricing looks very attractive today.

Conclusion: Reiterate Buy Rating

To conclude, LKQ can still demonstrate ongoing financial strength and maintaining revenues, supported by their strategic advantages in size and scale. Their proactive approach to market expansion through acquisitions and investments in technology could serve as a growth catalyst if the conditions remain favorable. Coupled with a consistent return of value to shareholders via dividends and share repurchases, I maintain a positive outlook on LKQ's stock, reinforcing a Buy recommendation despite known industry headwinds and further potential volatility.

One final thing I’ll leave you with - Seeking Alpha has a new nifty seasonality feature you can use to time your stock purchases and sales, if you do that sort of thing.

data.title: German Ifo Index Increases In May
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

German businesses remain cautiously optimistic. After the back-and-forth on US tariffs, German businesses seem to be focusing on the bright side of what could happen under the new German government, rather than fearing the downsides from ongoing uncertainty and trade tensions. This is, at least, the message that the just-released Ifo index seems to convey. Germany's most prominent leading indicator improved again and came in at 87.3 in May, from 86.9 in April. While the current assessment component weakened to 86.1, from 86.4 in April, expectations improved significantly to 88.9, from 87.4, the highest in a year.

Between nearer-term risks and longer-term opportunities

Taking a step back from high frequency data (and noise), the German economy remains in the middle of two seismic activities: a new government, which seems to lack strong ambition for structural reforms but will have access to unprecedented fiscal space for infrastructure and defence investments, and fundamental shifts in trade and geopolitics, including US tariffs. We think that, at least in the shorter run, the negatives will outweigh the positives. Not that we enjoy negative news, but we're currently witnessing several official forecasts for German growth this year converging with our long-held view of yet another year of stagnation. Yesterday’s forecasts from the Germany council of economic advisors was just another example.

Still, not everything is bad. The German economy actually grew in the first quarter, probably as a result of export frontloading, and industrial data pointed to some cyclical rebound at least before 'Liberation Day'. The tariff extravaganza since the beginning of April, however, will leave clear marks on the German economy. There is the direct impact, as even with the current 90-days-pause, tariffs are still higher than at the start of the year, but also the indirect impact via confidence and still high uncertainty. In the longer term, the announced fiscal stimulus will boost growth in Germany. Implemented in the right way, investment in infrastructure should lead to a cyclical upswing, at least. The caveat, however, remains that the fiscal measures alone – impressive as they might be – will do very little to improve the economy’s competitiveness. Modern infrastructure is essential for one of the world's largest economies, but it doesn't inherently drive innovation, sector transformation, or new growth opportunities. In this regard, we still expect tensions within the government about potential expenditure cuts and some structural reforms as the intended additional spending plans, other than infrastructure and defence, are hard to reconcile with the European fiscal rules.

The frustrating observation, however, is that the analysis of a stagnating economy with the need for investment and structural change is not new. While the discussion has been going on for years now, actual action to change the situation has been limited. In fact, due to the tensions within the last government, the elections and the new government just off the starting blocks, another year of economic policy standstill has passed. Last week, Chancellor Friedrich Merz announced that Germans should feel some positive changes by this summer. We agree that a quick implementation of policy and quick results will be needed but remain sceptical that the summer will bring significant change given the long implementation lags of public investment. Rather than seeing tangible improvements, Germans may find themselves noticing little more than old fax machines still lingering on the streets of Berlin. Public investment and structural changes simply take time.

All in all, today’s Ifo index comes as a positive surprise. Still, we caution against overly hasty optimism. There are currently more unknowns than knowns for the German economy, and we continue to expect another year of stagnation – which would mark the first time ever for Germany to go through three consecutive years without growth.
